paired_ppu_ttest_rel.description: H0: mean female PPU = mean male PPU (paired by row)

paired_ppu_wilcoxon.description: Wilcoxon signed-rank on paired female vs male PPU

city_pink_tax_ttest_ind.description: Welch t-test: pink_tax_pct Hyderabad vs Tokyo (independent)

city_pink_tax_mann_whitney.description: Mann–Whitney U on pink_tax_pct by city

m4_paper_city_category_retailer_overlap_.label: Report Table 5 — M4 (M3 + ingredient overlap, n=273)

m6_controls_price_match_size_hc3.formula: pink_tax_pct ~ C(city) + C(category) + C(retailer) + match_quality + log_mean_ppu + size_ratio

m7_full_with_ingredients_hc3.formula: pink_tax_pct ~ C(city) + C(category) + C(retailer) + match_quality + log_mean_ppu + size_ratio + ingredient_overlap_pct + jaccard_similarity

m8_logit_pink_positive.formula: pink_pos ~ C(city) + C(category) + C(retailer) + match_quality + log_mean_ppu + size_ratio

conclusions.summary: Hypothesis tests on paired PPUs and on pink_tax_pct, together with OLS and logit models, show where unadjusted and adjusted comparisons disagree: pooled city gaps can be small while fixed-effect models highlight channel/category confounding. Use the written conclusions for assignment-ready wording.
bullets: Paired PPU test (same row): female vs male price-per-unit t-test p=0.2004 (n=278). This asks whether women’s SKUs are priced higher per unit on average.
City comparison (unadjusted): Hyderabad mean pink tax 7.84%, Tokyo 18.09%, Welch t-test p=0.2421.
City-only OLS (HC3): Tokyo vs Hyderabad p=0.2428 (coef 10.25 pp)—matches pooled city means.
OLS with category + retailer FE (HC3): Tokyo coef 21.10 pp, p=0.0066 (significant at α=0.05). Interprets as conditional association, not raw city ranking.
Same FE model, SEs clustered by brand: Tokyo p=0.0015.
With category FE only + overlap: ingredient_overlap p=0.9299, jaccard p=0.9041.
Rich OLS (price level, match quality, size ratio controls): among continuous terms, largest |t| is log_mean_ppu (coef -6.7236, p=0.0988).
Full OLS including ingredients (n=273): ingredient_overlap_pct p=0.9670—even controlling for similarity, city/retailer/category structure remains in this sample.

Logit P(pink tax > 0): Tokyo coefficient (log-odds) ≈ 1.956 (OR≈7.069); HC0 SE for the city term is not finite (quasi-separation). Use OLS on pink_tax_pct for a stable city contrast; interpret logit for the continuous controls.
written_conclusions: Dataset size: 278 matched rows after cleaning. Pink tax % is defined from female vs male price per unit within each pair.
On average, the female SKU carries a 12.93% higher price per unit than the male SKU when averaged at the pair level (mean of pink_tax_pct); the two-sided t-test against 0 has p=0.003404.
By raw category means, the largest average pink-tax values in this sample appear in categories such as Hand Wash (mean 68.5%, n=7); use charts for the full distribution.

In an OLS model with category and retailer fixed effects (HC3 robust SEs), Tokyo (vs Hyderabad) is associated with about 21.1 percentage points additional pink tax on average (p=0.006646), holding those factors constant.

In the richer OLS that adds log mean PPU, size ratio, and match quality, the strongest continuous predictor by |t| is log_mean_ppu (p=0.09882). Ingredient overlap is added in the extended specification when non-missing.
limitations: Pairs are observational; promos and pack-size quirks affect pink_tax_pct.
City effects are confounded with retailer/currency context in places.
Logit and OLS can diverge because they target different estimands (level vs probability).
Ingredient fields are incomplete for some SKUs.
